headline: All majors underwater on 200-day lines; shorts are bleeding while longs hold conviction despite extreme fear.
paragraphs: The regime is uniformly bearish: all seven tracked assets trade below their 200-day simple moving averages, and none have printed a golden cross. Bitcoin sits 47.6 percent off its 12-month high; Ethereum 59.9 percent; Solana 68.2 percent; Cardano 82.6 percent. This is not a market in recovery; it is a market that has not yet found floor. The crowd knows it: fear and greed sits at 25, extreme fear, though it has touched 11 in the last month, so panic has visited more brutal ground. Total market capitalization rose 2.16 percent in 24 hours and Bitcoin dominance holds at 56.3 percent, but these minor lifts come against a backdrop of systematic underperformance across the entire major asset class.

Momentum and trend are not aligned; they are fighting. Bitcoin's RSI at 57.7 and Ethereum's at 68.0 both sit in neutral to slightly overbought territory, and both have MACD above signal line, which ought to suggest upward momentum. Yet neither asset is anywhere near its trend line. Ethereum is the tell: RSI of 68 in a market that sits 59.9 percent below the 12-month high is a divergence. The price is rallying inside a downtrend. Solana's MACD is below signal line while RSI sits at 55.8, a milder discrepancy but the same shape. When momentum brightens but price stays pinned below the long-term trend, the rally is either a bear trap or the opening frame of something larger. The 30-day realized volatility across the board is elevated, Solana at 52.8 percent and Cardano at 73.2 percent, which means the range is wide; the direction remains unclear.

Leverage positioning reveals a split narrative. Bitcoin's funding rate sits at 0.46 percent per 8 hours (annualized 5.0 percent), which is positive but not extreme; people want to hold long, but they are not desperate. Open interest is 2.016 billion dollars and the long/short ratio is 1.37, showing more longs than shorts but not by a landslide. The problem is in the liquidations: in 24 hours, 1,756 million dollars of longs went underwater but 10.996 billion dollars of shorts were liquidated. Shorts are the ones getting caught. The same pattern in Ethereum: the 24-hour window shows 927 million in long liquidations versus 13.5 billion in short liquidations. Solana even sharper: 565 thousand in longs, 1.016 million in shorts. Doge and XRP show the same slope. This is not a market where longs are bleeding; this is a market where the short book is fractured. When shorts liquidate into a downturn, the bounce is often sharp and painful for those who were betting on the fall.

Whale flows tell a coherent story: 173.5 million dollars moved off exchanges in 24 hours, all of it Bitcoin, and the direction was outflow. The biggest single move was 94.4 million Bitcoin leaving Coinbase institutional, followed by 82.6 million from OKX. Meanwhile, 113.7 million USDC came off Kraken and 108.1 million USDC moved onto Coinbase retail, meaning stablecoin holders are staged but dry powder shrank 1.91 percent over 30 days to 306.5 billion. The weekly net flow for the past four periods shows accumulation in the prior three weeks, then a small reversal (net 135 million off exchanges) most recently. Whales are not panicking out; they are moving coins to self-custody even as the weekly cadence softens. This behavior during extreme fear is classical: big holders do not chase price down, they secure their holdings.

Sentiment and the tape are inverted. The fear gauge is at 25; the money shows conviction. Spot ETF flows have printed 181.1 million into Bitcoin (latest, July 14) and 58.3 million into Ethereum, which is still inflow on a day when the crowd is terrified and all assets sit below their 200-day lines. Cumulative flows since inception are 51.086 billion into Bitcoin and 11.045 billion into Ethereum, meaning traditional finance has been a consistent buyer into weakness. That is not desperation; that is design. Network difficulty on Bitcoin is set to rise 4.2 percent at retarget, confirming that mining power remains stable even as the price sinks, another signal of underlying conviction.

Watch for a reclaim of the 200-day line on any of the majors, starting with Bitcoin. That would be the first structural break in the regime. Watch also whether the short liquidation cascade continues or abates: if shorts stop bleeding and the long/short ratio begins to compress, the narrative could reverse fast. Monitor stablecoin float: if it begins to grow again after its 30-day decline, that is new dry powder being staged and a behavioral shift. Finally, track whale flows: outflow off exchanges is accumulation behavior, but a reversal back onto exchanges in this same fear environment would signal a shift from hoarding to distribution, which would matter. Until one of those conditions flips, the read stays: deep bear market with momentum anomalies, short-book stress, and big money still buying.
